Summary: This study develops and assesses a radiomics-based prediction model using contrast-enhanced CT images to distinguish between T2 and T3 stages of laryngeal and hypopharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma (LHSCC). The model combines radiomic features with morphological imaging characteristics to create a nomogram, demonstrating good diagnostic performance and clinical usefulness for guiding treatment decisions.

Condition1.reason: The study includes manual delineation of ROIs on CT images for feature extraction, indicating segmentation.

Condition2.reason: The study mentions manual delineation of ROIs, so the segmentation is not fully automated.

Condition3.reason: The study extracts 851 radiomic features from CT images using the PyRadiomics package, which are hand-crafted features.

Condition4.reason: The study uses radiomic features in a CSV format, which is a tabular data format.

Condition5.reason: The study uses traditional machine learning classifiers (e.g., logistic regression) rather than an end-to-end deep learning approach.

Item1.reason: The study does not explicitly mention adherence to specific radiomics or machine learning checklists or guidelines like the CLEAR checklist.

Item2.reason: The study defines inclusion and exclusion criteria for patient enrollment, aiming to create a representative study population.

Item3.reason: The reference standard is histopathological examination, which is a high-quality reference standard.

Item4.reason: The study mentions that contrast-enhanced CT images were acquired by one of three scanners, but all scanners are at a single institution.

Item5.reason: The study uses contrast-enhanced CT images, which are a standard imaging technique in clinical practice for LHSCC staging.

Item6.reason: The study mentions that the details of the scanning parameters are described in Supplementary A1.

Item7.reason: The study specifies that histopathological examination was performed within 2 weeks after CT scanning, which is an appropriate interval.

Item8.reason: The study describes the delineation criteria in Supplementary A3 and mentions that the images were manually delineated and validated by two readers.

Item9.reason: The study uses manual segmentation, not fully automated segmentation, so this item is not applicable.

Item10.reason: The study mentions that the ROIs were delineated and validated by two readers, so the final segmentation in the test set was not produced by a single reader or automated tool.

Item11.reason: The study mentions standardization preprocessing of images and feature extraction using 3D Slicer software with the PyRadiomics package.

Item12.reason: The study uses the PyRadiomics package (version 3.0.1) for feature extraction, which is a standardized software.

Item13.reason: The study describes the feature types (original image features and wavelet decomposition features) and provides information on the feature extraction parameters.

Item14.reason: The study assesses the reproducibility of extracted features using ICCs and only uses features with ICCs > 0.75 for subsequent analyses.

Item15.reason: The study uses Pearson's correlation coefficient (PCC) for dimensionality reduction, which is a method for removing redundant features.

Item16.reason: The study does not explicitly discuss the appropriateness of dimensionality compared to data size or provide statistical justification.

Item17.reason: The study does not use an end-to-end deep learning pipeline, so this item is not applicable.

Item18.reason: The study randomly splits the data into training and test sets at a ratio of 7:3.

Item19.reason: The study uses the Kruskal-Wallis test to remove features showing significant statistical differences among different scanners, addressing potential confounding factors.

Item20.reason: The study uses AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity to assess the performance of the models.

Item21.reason: The study reports 95% confidence intervals (CI) for the AUC values.

Item22.reason: The Hosmer–Lemeshow goodness-of-fit test was performed to evaluate the calibration of the nomogram, and a calibration curve was plotted.

Item23.reason: The study does not compare the performance of a uni-parametric approach to the multi-parametric approach used.

Item24.reason: The study incorporates morphological imaging characteristics evaluated by radiologists into the nomogram and compares the net benefit using decision curve analysis (DCA).

Item25.reason: The study does not explicitly compare the performance of the radiomic model to a simple or classical statistical model.

Item26.reason: The study tests the model on an independent test set sampled from the same source as the training set.

Item27.reason: The study is a single-center study and does not include external testing with data from other institutions.

Item28.reason: The study does not mention any imaging, segmentation, clinical, or radiomics analysis data being shared with the public.

Item29.reason: The study does not mention any scripts related to automatic segmentation and/or modeling being shared with the public.

Item30.reason: The study does not mention the final model being shared in any form.
